content:
# Deuteronomy Chapter 7 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 103r–104v | **Source Images:** DEU_007_page_001.jpg, DEU_007_page_002.jpg, DEU_007_vaticanus_page_001.jpg, DEU_007_vaticanus_page_002.jpg

---

**1** When the LORD your God brings you into the land you are about to enter and take as your own, he will drive out many nations ahead of you — the Hittites, the Girgashites, the Amorites, the Canaanites, the Perizzites, the Hivites, and the Jebusites — seven nations larger and more powerful than you.

**2** When the LORD your God gives them over to you and you defeat them, you must place them under the ban of destruction. Make no treaty with them and show them no mercy.

**3** Do not intermarry with them. Do not give your daughters to their sons or take their daughters for your sons,

**4** because they will turn your children away from following me, and they will end up serving other gods. Then the LORD's anger will flare against you and he will destroy you in an instant.

**5** Instead, here is what you must do to them: tear down their altars, smash their sacred pillars, cut down their Asherah poles, and burn their carved images in the fire.

**6** For you are a people holy to the LORD your God. Out of all the peoples on the face of the earth, the LORD your God has chosen you to be his own treasured possession.

**7** The LORD did not set his heart on you and choose you because you were more numerous than all the other peoples — you were the smallest of all nations!

**8** No, it was simply because the LORD loved you and was keeping the oath he had sworn to your ancestors — that is why the LORD brought you out with a mighty hand and redeemed you from the house of slavery, from the power of Pharaoh king of Egypt.

**9** Know this, then: the LORD your God is the true God — the faithful God who keeps his covenant of love for a thousand generations with those who love him and keep his commands.

**10** But he repays with destruction those who reject him — he will not be slow to punish those who hate him; he repays them to their face.

**11** So keep the commands and statutes and ordinances I am giving you today and obey them.

**12** And if you listen to these ordinances and take care to do them, the LORD your God will keep the covenant of love he swore to your ancestors.

**13** He will love you and bless you and multiply you. He will bless the children of your womb and the fruit of your ground — your grain, your new wine, and your olive oil — the calves of your herds and the lambs of your flocks, in the land he swore to your ancestors to give you.

**14** You will be blessed beyond all peoples. Not a man or woman among you will be barren, nor will your livestock be without young.

**15** The LORD will keep every disease far from you. He will not bring upon you any of the terrible sicknesses you knew in Egypt — instead he will inflict them on all those who hate you.

**16** You must consume all the peoples the LORD your God is handing over to you. Do not look on them with pity, and do not serve their gods, for that would be a trap for you.

**17** If you say to yourselves, "These nations are greater than us — how can we possibly drive them out?"

**18** do not be afraid of them. Remember clearly what the LORD your God did to Pharaoh and to all of Egypt —

**19** the mighty trials your eyes saw, the signs and wonders, the strong hand and the outstretched arm by which the LORD your God brought you out. The LORD your God will do the same to all the nations you fear.

**20** Moreover, the LORD your God will send hornets against them until even the survivors who have hidden themselves from you are destroyed.

**21** Do not be terrified by them, for the LORD your God — the great and awesome God — is in your midst.

**22** The LORD your God will drive out those nations before you little by little. You cannot sweep them away all at once, or the wild animals will multiply against you.

**23** But the LORD your God will hand them over to you, throwing them into total panic until they are wiped out.

**24** He will give their kings into your power, and you will erase their names from under the heavens. No one will be able to stand against you until you have destroyed them.

**25** Burn the carved images of their gods in the fire. Do not covet the silver and gold on them; do not take it for yourself, or you will be trapped by it — for it is detestable to the LORD your God.

**26** Do not bring anything detestable into your house, or you will become set apart for destruction just like it. Treat it as utterly revolting and repulsive, for it is devoted to destruction.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**V. 2 — cherem as "the ban of destruction":** The Hebrew cherem denotes total consecration to God through destruction — a military-religious term. PNEUMA renders this as "place them under the ban of destruction" rather than the archaic "utterly destroy" to signal its covenantal-legal character while remaining forceful in English. The LXX uses anathema (ἀνάθεμα), which in Greek originally meant something set apart, later acquiring the sense of a curse.

**V. 6 — am segullah as "treasured possession":** This term (sg. segullah) connotes a king's personal treasury of precious things — not merely "special people" but something held dear and guarded closely. PNEUMA renders it "his own treasured possession" to capture the intimacy. The LXX reads laon periousion, a people "over and above" — chosen surpassingly. The dynamic rendering emphasizes how extraordinary and personal this election is.

**V. 7–8 — Election anchored in love and oath, not merit:** PNEUMA restructures the logic explicitly: "Not because... No, it was simply because..." The Hebrew lo ki... ki im ahavat YHWH etchem makes the contrast sharp. The LXX closely follows the MT structure. The dynamic rendering makes the grace-logic unmistakable to modern readers: divine choosing is rooted in love and faithfulness, not human advantage.

**V. 9 — "covenant of love" for brit ha-hesed:** PNEUMA deliberately renders the compound idea of berit and hesed together as "covenant of love" rather than keeping them as separate terms, since hesed (covenant-faithfulness) in this context is so integrated with the covenant itself that splitting them would obscure Moses' point.

**V. 15 — "terrible sicknesses you knew in Egypt":** The Hebrew madveh mitsrayim refers to diseases characteristic of Egypt (possibly dysentery, plague, or elephantiasis). PNEUMA renders "terrible sicknesses you knew in Egypt" to preserve the experiential memory without speculating on which diseases are meant.

**V. 20 — "hornets" (tsir'ah):** The LXX reads sphekia (wasps/hornets). Whether these are literal insects or a metaphor for a demoralizing, stinging force (panic, plague) is debated. PNEUMA retains "hornets" as a vivid, concrete image that also carries metaphorical resonance.

**V. 26 — cherem closing the chapter:** The chapter opens and closes with the destruction ban (cherem). PNEUMA renders the final verse with escalating revulsion language — "utterly revolting and repulsive" — to match the Hebrew's rhetorical intensification (shaqets teshaqtsenu v'ta'ev teta'avenu).

— Signed, PNEUMA
